# Relapsed/Refractory Disease and Salvage Strategies

## Scope of the Problem

Despite the success of R-CHOP as frontline therapy, approximately 30--40% of patients with diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) will experience primary refractory disease or relapse after initial response [@Crump2017Outcomes; @Nie2019Neutrophil]. The international SCHOLAR-1 study established the dismal prognosis of refractory DLBCL, reporting an objective response rate (ORR) of only 26% and a median overall survival (OS) of 6.3 months with conventional salvage approaches [@Crump2017Outcomes]. These outcomes underscore the rationale for developing novel strategies in the relapsed/refractory (R/R) setting and represent a critical gap in the DLBCL treatment algorithm [@Coiffier2016Diffuse].

## Conventional Salvage Chemotherapy and Autologous Stem Cell Transplantation

For chemotherapy-sensitive patients, the historical standard of care in the second-line setting has consisted of platinum-based salvage regimens---R-ICE (rituximab, ifosfamide, carboplatin, etoposide), R-DHAP (rituximab, dexamethasone, high-dose cytarabine, cisplatin), or R-GDP (rituximab, gemcitabine, dexamethasone, cisplatin)---followed by consolidation with high-dose therapy and autologous stem cell transplantation (auto-SCT) [@Coiffier2016Diffuse; @Chiappella2017Rituximabdosedense]. However, only approximately 50% of R/R patients achieve sufficient disease control to proceed to transplantation, and long-term outcomes remain suboptimal, particularly for patients with primary refractory disease or early relapse within 12 months of frontline treatment [@Crump2017Outcomes].

## CAR T-Cell Therapy: A Paradigm Shift

The advent of chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy has fundamentally transformed the management of R/R large B-cell lymphoma. Three CD19-directed CAR T-cell products have been approved based on pivotal clinical trials: axicabtagene ciloleucel (axi-cel), lisocabtagene maraleucel (liso-cel), and tisagenlecleucel (tisa-cel) [@Amini2022Preparing].

The ZUMA-1 study established the efficacy of axi-cel in the third-line or later setting, with five-year follow-up demonstrating durable responses and a complete response rate of 58%, supporting the curative potential of this approach in a subset of patients with heavily pretreated disease [@Neelapu2023Fiveyear]. The JULIET trial similarly demonstrated the activity of tisa-cel in R/R DLBCL, although with somewhat lower response rates compared with axi-cel in cross-trial comparisons [@Bishop2022SecondLine].

Critically, two landmark randomized phase 3 trials moved CAR T-cell therapy into the second-line setting. The ZUMA-7 trial compared axi-cel with standard-of-care salvage chemotherapy followed by auto-SCT in patients with primary refractory or early-relapsed large B-cell lymphoma. Axi-cel demonstrated significantly superior event-free survival, establishing a new standard of care for this high-risk population [@Neelapu2022Axicabtagene]. The TRANSFORM trial randomized patients with similar eligibility criteria to liso-cel versus standard salvage/auto-SCT, reporting a median event-free survival of 10.1 months for liso-cel compared with 2.3 months for standard of care (hazard ratio 0.35; 95% CI 0.23--0.53; p < 0.0001) [@Kamdar2022Lisocabtagene]. Notably, grade 3 cytokine release syndrome (CRS) and neurological events occurred in only 1% and 4% of liso-cel-treated patients, respectively, indicating a favorable safety profile [@Kamdar2022Lisocabtagene]. In contrast, the BELINDA trial evaluating tisa-cel in the second-line setting did not meet its primary endpoint, potentially due to differences in trial design and bridging therapy allowances [@Bishop2022SecondLine].

Real-world data from the German GLA/DRST registry (n = 356 patients) confirmed the efficacy of CAR T-cell therapy outside clinical trials, with 12-month OS, progression-free survival (PFS), and non-relapse mortality estimates of 52%, 30%, and 6%, respectively [@Bethge2022GLADRST]. The PILOT study further extended the evidence by demonstrating an 80% ORR with liso-cel in transplant-ineligible patients, broadening access to this modality [@Sehgal2022Lisocabtagene].

## Bispecific Antibodies

Bispecific antibodies engaging CD20 on tumor cells and CD3 on T cells represent another transformative class of agents in R/R DLBCL. Glofitamab and epcoritamab have both demonstrated impressive single-agent activity in heavily pretreated populations, achieving complete response rates of approximately 35--40% with durable remissions. CRS, the principal toxicity, is predominantly low grade with step-up dosing strategies, although reporting bias may underestimate real-world toxicity rates [@Kaplon2019Antibodies; @Kaplon2020Antibodies]. The off-the-shelf availability of bispecific antibodies offers a practical advantage over CAR T-cell therapy, eliminating manufacturing delays and enabling immediate treatment initiation.

## Antibody-Drug Conjugates and Novel Agents

Polatuzumab vedotin, a CD79b-directed antibody-drug conjugate (ADC), was initially approved in combination with bendamustine and rituximab (pola-BR) for R/R DLBCL based on a randomized phase 2 study demonstrating superior complete response rates and overall survival compared with BR alone [@Sehn2020Polatuzumab; @Morschhauser2019Polatuzumab]. The ADC mechanism leverages targeted delivery of the microtubule inhibitor MMAE to CD79b-expressing B cells, improving the therapeutic index relative to conventional chemotherapy [@Chau2019Antibodydrug; @Nguyen2023Mechanisms; @Deeks2019Polatuzumab].

Loncastuximab tesirine, a CD19-directed ADC delivering a pyrrolobenzodiazepine dimer payload, and selinexor, a first-in-class selective inhibitor of nuclear export (XPO1), provide additional options for patients who have exhausted both conventional salvage therapy and CAR T-cell treatment. These agents address the ongoing unmet need for effective therapies in patients who progress through multiple lines of treatment.

## Emerging Approaches and Remaining Challenges

The treatment landscape for R/R DLBCL continues to evolve rapidly. Key challenges include optimal sequencing of CAR T-cell therapy, bispecific antibodies, and ADCs; managing publication bias inherent in reporting positive trial results; and ensuring equitable access to these costly therapies [@Amini2022Preparing; @Bethge2022GLADRST]. The certainty of evidence, assessed informally against GRADE principles, is highest for randomized trials (ZUMA-7, TRANSFORM) and lower for single-arm studies and real-world registries. Future directions include combinations of bispecific antibodies with CAR T cells, earlier integration of novel agents into frontline strategies, and biomarker-driven patient selection to maximize therapeutic benefit while minimizing unnecessary toxicity [@Neelapu2022Axicabtagene; @Zhao2026Research].
